facts.t: Gertrude Ederle becomes the first woman to swim across the English Channel.
NBC launches as the first major American radio network.
Robert Goddard launches the world's first liquid-fueled rocket in Massachusetts.
The historic U.S. Route 66 is officially commissioned.
The federal government orders industrial alcohol poisoned to scare off Prohibition-era drinkers.
Charles Lindbergh completes the first solo nonstop transatlantic flight.
Babe Ruth hits a then-record 60 home runs in a single season.
Ford halts Model T production after 15 million cars to launch the Model A.
The Jazz Singer debuts as the first popular talkie feature film.
Bootlegger Al Capone rakes in tens of millions a year running Chicago's illegal alcohol trade.
Herbert Hoover wins the presidential election in a landslide.
Amelia Earhart becomes the first woman to cross the Atlantic by airplane.
Mickey Mouse makes his debut in the cartoon Steamboat Willie.
Sliced bread is sold commercially for the first time, in Chillicothe, Missouri.
Construction begins on the Art Deco Chrysler Building in New York City.
The Wall Street Crash of 1929 wipes out fortunes and triggers the Great Depression.
Al Capone's gang guns down rivals in the Prohibition-fueled St. Valentine's Day Massacre.
The first Academy Awards ceremony honors the best films of 1927-28.
The Museum of Modern Art opens its doors in New York City.
The Seeing Eye, America's first guide dog school, is founded.
Pluto is discovered by astronomer Clyde Tombaugh at Lowell Observatory.
Sinclair Lewis becomes the first American to win the Nobel Prize in Literature.
The Chrysler Building is completed, briefly the world's tallest building.
The Twinkie snack cake is invented in Illinois.
Grape growers legally sell wine-brick concentrate with labels warning exactly how not to ferment it into wine.
The Empire State Building opens, becoming the world's tallest building.
The Star-Spangled Banner is officially adopted as the U.S. national anthem.
Gangster Al Capone is convicted of federal income tax evasion.
Nevada legalizes casino gambling.
Jane Addams becomes the first American woman to win the Nobel Peace Prize.
The infant son of Charles Lindbergh is kidnapped in a case that grips the nation.
Franklin D. Roosevelt is elected president amid the Great Depression.
Amelia Earhart becomes the first woman to fly solo across the Atlantic.
Unemployed World War I veterans, the Bonus Army, march on Washington D.C.
The Democratic Party campaigns on repealing Prohibition, sensing the public's thirst for legal booze.
Prohibition ends when the 21st Amendment is ratified, legalizing alcohol again.
The Cullen-Harrison Act legalizes beer months before full Prohibition repeal, prompting long lines at breweries.
President Franklin D. Roosevelt delivers his first radio fireside chat to reassure the nation.
King Kong climbs the Empire State Building in the smash-hit monster film.
The Civilian Conservation Corps is created as part of the New Deal, putting young men to work.
Outlaws Bonnie and Clyde are killed in a police ambush in Louisiana.
Bank robber John Dillinger is shot dead by federal agents outside a Chicago movie theater.
Massive dust storms devastate the Great Plains during the Dust Bowl.
Alcatraz Island opens as a maximum-security federal prison.
The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission is established to regulate Wall Street.
President Roosevelt signs the Social Security Act, creating the modern safety net.
The first canned beer, Krueger's Cream Ale, goes on sale in Virginia.
Babe Ruth plays his final major league baseball game.
The China Clipper makes the first scheduled transpacific airmail flight.
Parker Brothers begins selling the board game Monopoly nationwide.
Jesse Owens wins four gold medals at the Berlin Olympics, undercutting Nazi propaganda.
Franklin D. Roosevelt wins re-election in a historic landslide.
The Hoover Dam is completed on the Colorado River.
Margaret Mitchell publishes her Civil War epic novel, Gone with the Wind.
Life magazine relaunches as a pioneering photojournalism weekly.
The airship Hindenburg bursts into flames while landing in New Jersey.
Aviator Amelia Earhart vanishes over the Pacific during her attempt to fly around the world.
San Francisco's Golden Gate Bridge opens to traffic.
Disney releases Snow White and the Seven Dwarfs, the first full-length animated feature film.
Congress passes the Marihuana Tax Act, effectively criminalizing cannabis nationwide.
Orson Welles' radio dramatization of The War of the Worlds sparks nationwide panic.
Superman makes his debut in Action Comics #1, launching the superhero genre.
The Fair Labor Standards Act establishes the first federal minimum wage.
Underdog racehorse Seabiscuit defeats War Admiral in a legendary match race.
Kate Smith debuts Irving Berlin's God Bless America on her radio show.
Gone with the Wind premieres in Atlanta, becoming a landmark Hollywood epic.
The Wizard of Oz premieres, dazzling audiences with Technicolor.
The New York World's Fair opens, showcasing the World of Tomorrow.
Marian Anderson sings at the Lincoln Memorial after being denied a stage at Constitution Hall.
Little League Baseball is founded in Williamsport, Pennsylvania.
Franklin D. Roosevelt wins an unprecedented third presidential term.
The McDonald brothers open their first hamburger stand in San Bernardino, California.
Igor Sikorsky flies the first practical helicopter in the United States.
Congress passes the nation's first peacetime military draft.
Nylon stockings go on sale nationwide, sparking shopping frenzies.
Japan's attack on Pearl Harbor draws the United States into World War II.
Joe DiMaggio hits safely in a record 56 consecutive games.
Ted Williams finishes the season batting .406, the last player to hit .400.
Mount Rushmore's presidential sculpture is completed after 14 years of work.
Orson Welles' Citizen Kane, later hailed as a cinematic masterpiece, premieres.
Executive Order 9066 leads to the forced internment of Japanese Americans.
The U.S. Navy's victory at the Battle of Midway turns the tide of the Pacific War.
The top-secret Manhattan Project is established to build an atomic bomb.
Bing Crosby records White Christmas, which becomes the best-selling single in history.
Wonder Woman gets her own ongoing comic book series.
Racial tensions explode into the Zoot Suit Riots in Los Angeles.
The Pentagon, the world's largest office building, is completed.
Rodgers and Hammerstein's Oklahoma! opens on Broadway, reinventing the musical.
Norman Rockwell publishes his iconic Four Freedoms paintings.
The government briefly bans sliced bread to conserve wartime resources.
Allied forces storm the beaches of Normandy on D-Day.
President Roosevelt signs the G.I. Bill, reshaping postwar American life.
Delegates at Bretton Woods design the postwar global monetary system.
Franklin D. Roosevelt is elected to an unprecedented fourth term.
The Smokey Bear wildfire-prevention campaign is launched.
Japan surrenders aboard the USS Missouri, ending World War II.
President Franklin D. Roosevelt dies in office; Harry Truman succeeds him.
The U.S. drops atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki.
The United Nations is founded to preserve postwar peace.
Naval engineer Richard James's Slinky toy is introduced and sells out in 90 minutes.
ENIAC, the first general-purpose electronic computer, is unveiled to the public.
Millions of returning GIs kick off the postwar Baby Boom.
Jackie Robinson breaks the minor-league color line playing for the Montreal Royals.
Dr. Benjamin Spock publishes his massively influential baby-and-child-care guide.
Frank Capra's It's a Wonderful Life premieres in theaters.
Jackie Robinson breaks Major League Baseball's color barrier with the Brooklyn Dodgers.
A mysterious crash near Roswell, New Mexico, fuels decades of UFO conspiracy theories.
Test pilot Chuck Yeager becomes the first person to break the sound barrier.
Tennessee Williams' A Streetcar Named Desire opens on Broadway.
Bell Labs scientists invent the transistor, birthing modern electronics.
Harry Truman pulls off a stunning upset over Thomas Dewey, embarrassing pollsters.
President Truman signs an executive order desegregating the U.S. armed forces.
The McDonald brothers retool their restaurant into a fast, assembly-line Speedee Service System.
Columbia Records introduces the 33 1/3 rpm long-playing vinyl record.
Alfred Kinsey publishes his controversial report on American male sexuality.
The United States joins Canada and Western European allies in founding NATO.
Bouncing putty, soon rebranded as Silly Putty, first appears as a novelty toy in a catalog.
The first Emmy Awards ceremony honors excellence in television.
Jackie Robinson wins the National League MVP award.
Arthur Miller's Death of a Salesman opens on Broadway and wins the Pulitzer Prize.
North Korean forces invade South Korea, drawing in U.S. troops as the Korean War begins.
Senator Joseph McCarthy ignites the Red Scare with sweeping communist-infiltration claims.
Charles Schulz's comic strip Peanuts, starring Charlie Brown, debuts.
Diners Club issues the first modern credit card, letting members charge restaurant tabs.
The musical Guys and Dolls opens on Broadway.
